---
title: "Structured dependence: phylogeny and space roadmap"
output: rmarkdown::html_vignette
vignette: >
  %\VignetteIndexEntry{Structured dependence: phylogeny and space roadmap}
  %\VignetteEngine{knitr::rmarkdown}
  %\VignetteEncoding{UTF-8}
---

```{r, include = FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(collapse = TRUE, comment = "#>")
```

Phylogenetic and spatial dependence are core extensions. The first fitted path
is intercept-only phylogenetic structure in the univariate Gaussian location
formula. Spatial fields, phylogenetic slopes, phylogenetic scale terms, and
bivariate structured covariance blocks remain staged extensions.

The mathematical bridge is simple:

```text
eta_d = X_d beta_d + Z_d z
z ~ MVN(0, sigma_z^2 K)
```

For phylogenetic models, `K` is a tree-derived matrix `A`. For spatial models,
`K` is a distance-derived matrix `M`, or a sparse SPDE/GMRF precision that
represents the same idea computationally. The package should teach these as
one family of structured effects attached to a distributional parameter.

Implemented phylogenetic syntax:

```r
drmTMB(
  bf(
    y ~ x1 + phylo(1 | species, tree = tree),
    sigma ~ x1
  ),
  family = gaussian(),
  data = dat
)
```

This fits a phylogenetic random intercept in the location model. The public
`phylo()` term requires an ultrametric tree with positive branch lengths so
`drmTMB` can build and validate the Hadfield and Nakagawa sparse augmented
A-inverse path internally.

Later phylogenetic random slopes should extend the same grammar:

```r
bf(
  y ~ x1 + phylo(1 + x1 | species, tree = tree),
  sigma ~ x1
)
```

This would estimate phylogenetic covariance among species-specific intercepts
and slopes. It is planned, not implemented, and should come after more
intercept-only recovery and comparator checks.

Planned spatial syntax:

```r
drmTMB(
  bf(
    y ~ depth + temp + spatial(1 | site, coords = coords),
    sigma ~ temp
  ),
  family = gaussian(),
  data = dat
)
```

This syntax is planned, not implemented.

The spatial term is deliberately parallel to the phylogenetic term:

```r
phylo(1 | species, tree = tree)
spatial(1 | site, coords = coords)
```

The mathematical role is the same: add a structured Gaussian random intercept
to a distributional predictor. The computational route differs. `phylo()`
starts from an ultrametric tree with branch lengths and builds a sparse
A-inverse; `spatial()` will start from coordinates or a mesh and build an
SPDE/GMRF precision.

The computational plan is to keep using sparse A-inverse tricks for phylogeny
and add SPDE/GMRF structure for spatial fields, with careful provenance notes
for any code selectively ported from `gllvmTMB`.

Structured phylogenetic and spatial random slopes will be staged more
conservatively than ordinary grouped random slopes. The first structured-effect
target should be intercept-only `mu`; the next should be one structured `mu`
slope. Small sets of structured slopes, interaction slopes, and structured
effects in `sigma` or `rho12` should wait until simulation recovery shows that
the simpler models are identifiable.

In meta-analysis this becomes especially transparent:

```text
e ~ MVN(0, V)                         # known sampling covariance
p_species ~ MVN(0, sigma_phylo^2 A)   # phylogenetic structure
l_location ~ MVN(0, sigma_space^2 M)  # spatial structure
```

The same design will let `drmTMB` support structured Gaussian meta-analysis
while making ecological and evolutionary meta-analyses straightforward.

The phylogenetic flagship is the location-scale model: trait means and residual
SDs can each have phylogenetic structure. In bivariate models this allows
several structured correlations, including mean-mean, scale-scale, and
mean-scale phylogenetic correlations.

`drmTMB` then adds the location-coscale extension:

```r
drmTMB(
  formula = drm_formula(
    mu1 = log_body_mass ~ lifestyle + phylo(1 | species, tree = tree),
    mu2 = log_litter_size ~ lifestyle + phylo(1 | species, tree = tree),
    sigma1 = ~ lifestyle,
    sigma2 = ~ lifestyle,
    rho12 = ~ lifestyle
  ),
  family = c(gaussian(), gaussian()),
  data = mammals
)
```

This syntax is planned, not implemented. It captures the intended extension of
phylogenetic location-scale models: after modelling mean and variance, test
whether the residual association between two traits changes with ecological
predictors such as lifestyle.

The residual `rho12` term remains distinct from the phylogenetic or spatial
correlation matrices. `rho12` is the residual coupling between two responses at
the observation level; `A` and `M` describe dependence among species or places.
In future bivariate structured models, `drmTMB` should also estimate
level-specific correlations such as phylogenetic mean-mean correlations,
non-phylogenetic species correlations, spatial field correlations, and
mean-scale correlations. These are not residual `rho12`; they should be
reported as structured or group-level covariance summaries.
